original_abstract:
Village Institutes represent a landmark implementation of education policy during the early years of the Turkish Republic. Originally established in 1940 to train village teachers, the mandates of these institutions expanded in response to the growing national demand for healthcare personnel. By 1943, the curriculum was restructured to include Health Branches, transforming the institutes into centers that produced dedicated village health officers capable of serving rural populations. 

This study bridges historical gaps by engaging directly with four retired village health officers—the last of whom graduated in 1951—through structured interviews and the collection of physical archives. The primary data includes a diverse range of original documents: student identity cards, period-specific textbooks, diplomas, official authorizations for urban service, military health service certificates, and anesthesia specialization permits. 

By analyzing these findings alongside existing literature, this research demonstrates that village health officers received a high-quality education tailored to the specific medical needs of rural areas, playing a pivotal role in the protection and promotion of public health during their tenure. Furthermore, this study shines a light on a professional group often overlooked in academic discourse. By documenting these nuanced details, the research offers a critical perspective on the intersection of educational philosophy, health policy, and social structure during a formative era of the Republic.
humanized_abstract:
Village Institutes were a new experiment in the application of the educational policy of the Republic. Founded in 1940 for the training of village teachers, they were modified in 1943 to meet the growing need of the country for health personnel and were turned into schools preparing health graduates for the function of village health nurse specialising in the health care of the villages.

The article fills a gap in the historical research on the colonial period in Malawi by interviewing four retired village health officers (VHOS) that are the last surviving members of the school that graduated in 1951. The methodological framework consists of interviews and a physical collection of historical data. The main data source consists of primary documents which are collected from the respondents including student identity cards, colonial medical texts for tropical diseases, graduated certificates, regulatory documents for urban health, certificates from the colonial military health services, and permits for specialisation in anaesthesia.

By relating this information to that found in the scientific literature, the research concludes that the education received by village health officers was highly adapted to the needs of health in rural environments and thus played an important role in the health protection and promotion efforts conducted in these areas during this period. This study, therefore, is a precious source of information concerning the group of village health officers who are frequently ignored in the scientific literature. Providing detailed information that brings new insights on pedagogical approaches, health policies and social conditions in the decisive years of the modern Republic, this work fills a gap in the literature regarding this particular historical period.
